title: Self-Esteem and Competition
author: Gilabert

opening_move.description: Self-esteem is a very important good; competition is widespread and commonly linked to self-esteem (people seek it through competitive success). But empirical research suggests competition has a strong tendency to undermine self-esteem. How might we foster a desirable distribution of self-esteem in the face of difficulties created by competition?
anchored_in: empirical psychology of self-esteem and competition
opening_move.stakes_claimed: A solidaristic path — identifying mechanisms that support individuals' self-esteem while imposing limits on competition — is preferable to either self-denial or uncritical celebration of the rat race.

theoretical_positioning.description: The paper draws on social psychology of self-esteem and competition to identify ten mechanisms for supporting self-esteem while limiting competition (solidarity, recognition, intrinsic motivation, etc.), then provides prudential and moral arguments for implementing them.

epistemic_mode.qualitative_methods: Review of empirical social psychology research on self-esteem and competition; no original data collection.
data_sources: Social psychology literature on self-esteem (Rosenberg, Crocker)
Empirical studies on competition and self-esteem
epistemic_mode.notes: The empirical psychology research is supportive: it establishes that competition tends to undermine self-esteem (the diagnostic premise), but the normative argument (what mechanisms to use) is independently derived through philosophical analysis. This is normative_with_qualitative_empirical (supportive) — the empirical component provides the diagnostic foundation but not the normative prescription.

confidence_notes: Type 5 (normative argument with empirical diagnosis, supportive variant): the empirical psychology establishes the problem (competition undermines self-esteem), the normative argument proposes the solution (ten mechanisms). Not constitutive because the normative prescription is independent of the specific empirical findings.
